schools.summary: Ellicott renders the heading 'as to courses, to porters,' counts 4,000 Levites set apart (1 Chr 23:6), and fixes Meshelemiah (=Shelemiah, =Shallum) as a Korhite of the Kohathite stock, with 'Asaph' an abbreviation of Ebiasaph.

Matthew Henry lifts the office into meditation: the porters guarded every avenue, directed worshippers, sent back the unclean, and prefigure the ministers who hold the keys of the kingdom (Matt. 16:19).

Clarke lays out the four porter-classes, one to each of the four cardinal gates (east Shelemiah, north Zechariah, south Obed-edom, west Shuppim and Hosah), with about a thousand men under each captain.
Gill defines the doorkeepers' work — opening and shutting the doors, barring the impure, guarding the vessels — and notes David divided them into waiting-classes like the singers.
Ellicott reads the seven 'sons' of Meshelemiah as seven porter-guilds; Keil and Delitzsch place him among the Kohathite Korahites and note the singular verb 'was born' before a plural offspring.
Gill gives the plain succession of birth — Zechariah the firstborn and six more in their order.

themes: doorkeeping entailed from father to son

schools.summary: Ellicott alone comments, glossing Jehohanan ('Jah bestowed,' the full form of Johanan/John) and Elioenai ('mine eyes are toward Jehovah,' cf. Ps. 123:1) as devout full forms drawn from an old source, while Elam and the others pass unremarked.

themes: the silence of the other witnesses

schools.summary: Gill recalls Obed-edom as the ark's former host and notes his eight sons, now porters, set down by birth.
Ellicott resolves the pedigree: Obed-edom, 'son of Jeduthun' (1 Chr 16:38), is a Korhite Kohathite, not a Merarite, since this Jeduthun was not the chief musician.
Henry makes the household a lesson in reward: he who reverenced the ark in his own tent is repaid by his sons' custody of it in God's house — trusty in little, trusted with more (cf. Ps. 119:56).

themes: a household drawn into God's service

schools.summary: Ellicott reads Obed-edom's eight sons as a litany of thanks (Shemaiah 'Jah hath heard,' Sacar 'reward,' cf. Gen. 15:1), echoing 1 Chr 13:14; Keil and Delitzsch gather the eight sons and grandsons into the sixty-two of v.8.

Clarke, with the Targum, grounds the blessing in the ark that lodged in Obed-edom's house; Wesley makes it a maxim — the building up of families is owing to the divine blessing, a great gift when the children serve God.
Henry: eight sons, 'for God blessed him' — the multiplying of a household is a gift of heaven.

themes: the ark's blessing on the house

schools.summary: Ellicott reads 'that ruled' as the abstract noun mimshal ('the authorities,' cf. Dan. 11:3), making these the ruling lords of their clan, and refers 'mighty men of valour' to his note on 1 Chr 9:13.

Clarke makes the porters a military guard for the gates; Wesley explains the recurring 'valour' by the office's real demands — heavy doors (one needing twenty men), keeping guard, barring the unclean, and later judging Israel (v.20).
Gill identifies Shemaiah as Obed-edom's firstborn.

themes: the porters as a military guard

schools.summary: Ellicott judges the text corrupt: 'Obed, Elzabad' probably a single wrecked name (Obed-el/Abdiel) with the conjunction lost, and 'his brethren' likewise missing an 'and' — a correction that, at v.12, yields the significant total of twenty-four porter-courses.
Gill passes the difficulty, noting only that this Shemaiah is Obed-edom's firstborn.

Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott read the sixty-two as sons and grandsons together, resolve the singular 'man of might' in apposition to 'all,' suspect a lost 'every' (cf. 1 Chr 10:12), and compare the porters' 'sons and brethren' to the musicians' (1 Chr 25:9).
Gill notes the sixty-two include the grandsons, the whole three generations gathered into one able company.

Keil and Delitzsch total the ninety-three heads (18 + 62 + 13) over the 4,000 doorkeepers, harmonizing the 212 of 1 Chr 9:22 and the 68 of 1 Chr 16:38 as figures from other times; Ellicott reads the verse as a supplement to v.2 and counts eighty Korhite families.
Gill sums the eighteen as seven sons plus eleven brethren or kinsmen.

Keil and Delitzsch explain Simri was made chief because Hosah's firstborn had died without descendants, so no family held the birthright; Ellicott adds it may mean the eldest family died out or none could prove seniority. Hosah's house numbered thirteen.
Wesley: Simri was made chief not in inheriting the estate (the law forbade it) but in this service, for which he was better qualified than his elder brother.
Henry: a younger son made chief, not in the inheritance (forbidden by Deut. 21:16) but in an office requiring personal fitness.

themes: the birthright untouched by the law

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch complete the ninety-three chief men (adding Hosah's thirteen) and read the verse as the pivot into v.12, where the named heads prove to be the very classes of doorkeepers committed to the watches of the house.
Gill breaks the thirteen into four sons and nine brethren or kinsmen of the Merarite Hosah.

themes: the roster of the gates completed

schools.summary: Ellicott shows the named heads are the porter-courses and, counting the houses (with Obed-Elzabad as one name, per v.7), reaches twenty-four courses matching the musicians of 1 Chr 25; Keil and Delitzsch confirm the watches were committed to these heads to minister in the house.
Wesley: the porters had 'wards' answerable to the other Levites, divided into twenty-four courses like the priests and singers.
Gill: the named men were the principal porters, divided into classes serving week by week in turn.
Clarke: none could choose his gate — each took it by lot; Wesley: times and places fixed not by age or dignity but by lot, that each gate's charge be known.

Ellicott stresses 'small and great alike' — posts assigned without distinction of rank — and describes the four-square, compass-oriented sanctuary (cf. the kings 'of the four quarters'); Keil and Delitzsch describe the lot cast for each of the four doors.
Gill: the lot determined at which gate each should stand.

Ellicott makes the east gate the post of honour given the senior house of Shelemiah (=Meshelemiah, =Shallum), and reads 'a wise counsellor' of Zechariah as an undesigned 'mark of truth' drawn from an older source; Keil and Delitzsch record the title but confess its reason unknown.
Gill identifies Shelemiah with Meshelemiah at the east gate, later called the gate Shushan from the city portrayed on it.

themes: the east gate as post of honour

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott define Beth-ha-asuppim (cf. Neh. 12:25) as the temple storehouse or magazine near the south door, with two entrances; Ellicott faults the Vulgate's 'council of elders.'
Clarke allows 'the house of the collections' for stores and offerings, but Wesley renders it 'of gatherings,' thinking it named from the assembly of elders who met there to consult on temple affairs.
Gill sets the south side where the two gates of Huldah later stood.

sides.position: a storehouse or magazine for the temple's provisions and property
a place of gathering, named from the assembly of elders who met there

themes: goods of the sanctuary under guard

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott judge 'Shuppim' a scribal echo of 'Asuppim' (no such warder in vv.1-11), read 'to Hosah westward,' derive Shallecheth from 'casting down' (Isa. 6:13; cf. the dung-gate, Neh. 3:13), and give two readings of 'ward against ward.'

Clarke and Wesley make Shallecheth the gate through which the temple's offal and ashes were carried out, on the private western quarter; Wesley reads 'ward against ward' as gate answering gate, west against east.
Gill takes 'Shuppim' as a real but otherwise unknown man sharing the western charge with Hosah.

debates.question: Is 'Shuppim' an original name or a copyist's error?

sides.position: a dittography of the preceding 'Asuppim,' to be dropped
Hosah guarded two opposite posts — the west gate and Shallecheth facing it
the general symmetry of the gates, each ward set over against another

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch tally twenty-four daily guard-stations (six east, four north, four south, four at the stores, six west with v.18), noting more than twenty-four men were needed for relief — but they divide on whether the number mirrors the twenty-four courses.
Clarke and Wesley explain the east's six as the better guard for the chief and most frequented gate of entrance.
Gill: the eastern gate, the way of entrance, required more porters.

debates.question: Do the twenty-four daily guard-stations correspond to the twenty-four porter-courses?

sides.position: only a deceptive appearance of a connection; the analogy proves nothing
a striking correspondence — the daily warders being chiefs with their companies

schools.summary: Ellicott, after Gesenius, reads Parbar (cf. Parwarim, 2 Kings 23:11) as a Persian 'summer-house' — an open colonnade of cells round the court — and completes the count of twenty-four daily warders (chiefs with companies); Keil and Delitzsch keep the tally (four at the causeway, two at Parbar) while leaving the sense uncertain.
Gill reports Lightfoot's view that Parbar (Parvar) means 'suburbs,' agreeing with a western gate leading to the suburbs in Josephus.

sides.position: a suburb or outer precinct on the west
an open colonnade or 'summer-house' of cells round the court

schools.summary: Ellicott presses the subscription's implication — only the Kohathite (Korahite) and Merarite houses served as warders, the Gershonites excluded; Keil and Delitzsch read it as the Chronicler's closing statement to that effect.
Gill traces the two lines — Kore a grandson of Kohath, Merari a brother of Kohath — from whom alone the porters came.

Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch judge 'Ahijah' doubtful — unattested in 1 Chr 23:7, unaccompanied by a family, and yielding 'their brethren' by one added letter (so the LXX) — and distinguish the two funds; Ellicott ties the house-treasures to Exod. 30:11, Lev. 27, Num. 18:16.

Henry makes the altar's daily stores a type of the plenty of the Father's house — in Christ the hidden treasures of wisdom and unsearchable riches — and notes the treasurers set to guard against moth, rust, and thief.
Wesley distinguishes the sacred vessels/treasures for the house's maintenance from things freely dedicated; Clarke (with Jarchi) marks the first as where the oblation-money was kept.
Gill keeps the received text, reading 'of the Levites' of the rest — Gershonites, Amramites, Izharites, Hebronites.

debates.question: Is 'Ahijah' (v.20) an original proper name or a corruption?

sides.position: a corruption of 'their brethren' (so the LXX), to be read of the other Levites
kept as the received text, read simply 'of the Levites' / the rest of them

themes: stores of the house of God
a type of the Father's plenty

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch unpack the compressed Hebrew — the Gershonite house of Laadan, headed by Jehieli, whose sons Zetham and Joel held the house-treasures — clearing it by 1 Chr 23:7, and take the very crabbedness as a mark of an old record.
Gill identifies Laadan with Libni, the elder Gershonite line.

Keil and Delitzsch confirm Zetham and Joel (of Jehieli, from Laadan) over the house-treasures and use the verse as the hinge to the Kohathite branches who will keep the dedicated things, under Shebuel.
Gill notes plainly that Zetham and Joel were both sons of Jehieli.

Ellicott reads the four Kohathite clans as a heading governing the rest of the chapter (Amramites vv.24-28, Izharites v.29, Hebronites vv.30-32; cf. 1 Chr 23:12); Keil and Delitzsch register them as the families from whom the stewards and officers come.
Gill traces the four from the sons of Kohath and notes the Uzzielites are never afterward mentioned.
Wesley accounts for the missing Uzzielites as either extinct (heard of only here and Num. 3:27) or lacking any man then fit to be trusted.

themes: the Chronicler's honesty to his sources

schools.summary: Ellicott makes the comptroller's office hereditary in this Amramite house and calls Shebuel 'prince' (nagid, cf. 1 Chr 5:2) over both funds of v.20; Keil and Delitzsch keep to the plain descent from Moses through Gershom.

Gill notes this the first of Moses's posterity in honoured office, made (per Jarchi and Kimchi) chief over all treasures and treasurers, and rejects the Targum's Jonathan identification as 'very wrong.'
Clarke, with the Targum, identifies Shebuel with Jonathan who 'returned to God,' made chief treasurer by David for his skill in money matters.

debates.question: Is Shebuel the same as Jonathan (the wandering Levite)?

sides.position: yes — Jonathan, reclaimed and made chief treasurer

themes: a descendant of Moses in office
Moses's seed as stewards, not priests

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch trace Eliezer's line (Rehabiah, Jeshaiah, Joram, Zichri, Shelomith), call these the 'brethren' of Shebuel as fellow-descendants of Moses, and distinguish this Amramite Shelomith from the Gershonite (1 Chr 23:9) and Izharite namesakes — all to establish who the treasurer of v.26 is.
Gill gives the succession from Eliezer, the other son of Moses, down to Shelomith.

Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott distinguish this Shelomith from his namesakes (1 Chr 23:9, 23:18), lay out the dedicators (David, chief fathers, captains of thousands/hundreds, commanders-in-chief like Abner and Joab), and correct 'to the princes' to 'and the princes' (cf. 1 Chr 29:6).
Gill identifies Shelomith of Eliezer's line as keeper of the consecrated gifts.
Clarke gathers the near-universal custom of giving God a share of spoils (Virgil, Servius, Jupiter the Spoiler); Wesley glosses 'maintain' simply as 'repair.'

Henry draws the sacred lesson the pagans shadowed — Abraham's tithe to Melchizedek, the officers' oblation (Num. 31:50) — that the more God bestows the more he expects; men of war must honour God with their spoils.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch read 'maintain' as 'make strong'/'preserve in good condition' (the verb meaning 'repair' at 2 Kings 12:8), an explanatory parenthesis on the spoils of war.
Gill notes the temple was not yet built; the spoils were vowed to raise, repair, and furnish it, comparing Rome's dedications to Jupiter Feretrius after Romulus.

debates.question: Does 'to maintain the house' mean to repair it or to strengthen/endow it?

sides.position: to make it strong / keep it in good condition

themes: the more God gives, the more he expects

schools.summary: Ellicott resumes the dedicators from v.26, glosses 'the seer' as the old word for prophet (1 Sam. 9:9), roots the custom in the Law (Num. 31:22; Josh. 6:19; cf. 2 Sam. 8:11), and explains how the slowly gathered spoils funded the temple (1 Chr 22:14) under Shelomith's hand.
Gill takes Samuel's dedicated spoil to have been won in his battle with the Philistines.

themes: 'the seer' the old word for prophet
how the temple's wealth was gathered

schools.summary: Henry makes magistracy a divine ordinance for the church as truly as ministry, has the Levites judge alongside the princes as the law's true students, and calls happy the kingdom whose sacred and civil interests are interwoven.
Ellicott defines the 'outward business' as officers (scribes) and judges — the 6,000 of 1 Chr 23:4 — perhaps including tithe- and tax-collection (cf. Neh. 11:16).

Clarke (with Jarchi) takes 'outward business' literally — timber, stone, ploughing the sanctuary's fields; Wesley reads it judicially, Levites as judges over the people since Israel's common law was the law of God.

sides.position: manual labour outside the city — timber, hewing stone, ploughing the sanctuary's fields
the civil and judicial administration of Israel — officers and judges

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch fix 'officers' as oversight/superintendence (LXX 'inspection'), rejecting Bertheau's 'tribute'; Ellicott infers from 'on the other side Jordan westward' (cf. Josh. 5:1; 22:7) that the source was written east of the river, perhaps at Babylon.

Clarke splits the charge into ecclesiastical ('business of the LORD') and civil ('service of the king'), drawing the lesson that Church and state should mutually support; Wesley details gathering temple monies and executing God's laws as the king's eyes and hands.
Gill's arithmetic (1,600 Izharites + 1,700 here + 2,700 beyond Jordan) makes up the 6,000 officers and judges of 1 Chr 23:4.

themes: God's business and the king's service
a hint of an exilic source

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch explain the parenthesis: the Hebronites, sought out in David's fortieth year, yielded valiant men at Jazer of Gilead (a Merarite Levite city, Josh. 21:39), and were set over the east-Jordan tribes under their head Jeriah.
Gill names Jerijah chief of the Hebronites, the posterity of Hebron son of Kohath.
Wesley marks the fortieth year as David's last, all these orders made near his end, and draws the lesson to do good the more as the day approaches.

themes: sought out at Jazer of Gilead

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch give the full sense — 2,700 chief fathers set over Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh 'for every matter pertaining to God, and affairs of the king' — completing the twofold sacred-and-civil charge across the Jordan.
Gill notes Jerijah's brethren were so many principal men, of fortitude and courage, set as heads over the trans-Jordan tribes.

themes: the twenty-seven hundred over the east
God's matters and the king's affairs
